method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima is Japanese and is translated as “western island”.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was initially named Rosario Island.[3] It retained that name until it was later renamed Nishinoshima.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is the emergent summit of a large submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter, forming part of the volcanic front of the Izu–Bonin arc along the north–south-trending Shichitō-Iwojima Ridge.[1][4] Volcanism in the region is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate along the Ogasawara Trench.[4] The volcanic edifice includes the island-forming main peak and two submarine peaks to the west and south, with a small cone on the northeast flank interpreted as a possible satellite cone.[4]

The island has historically been described as andesitic, with reported SiO₂ contents of ejecta ranging from 51.8 to 60.1 wt %.[1] Later geochemical work showed that the 2020 eruption also produced basalt, basaltic andesite, and dacite, while its andesites were similar to those erupted since 1973.[3] Nishinoshima andesitic primary magmas have been interpreted as mantle-derived melts produced by shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] Basalt erupted in 2020 is comparable to older basalts from surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magma may have been generated throughout the volcano’s history but reached the island summit as an eruption center in 2020.[3]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the seafloor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the pre-existing island, initially as submarine phreatomagmatic explosions.[1] By September 1973 the activity had formed a new island, and five craters subsequently produced volcanic blocks and lava flows as the eruption migrated in a semicircular pattern.[1] Although severe marine erosion removed much of the newly built volcanic edifices, redistributed deposits eventually linked the old and new land areas and closed the mouth of the intervening bay by 1982.[1]

The 2013–2020 activity has been divided into four eruptive episodes, monitored mainly by satellites, aircraft, and research vessels because of the island’s remoteness.[3][4] Episode 1 lasted about two years in 2013–2015, while Episode 2 in 2017 lasted about 120 days and had a lower estimated average effusion rate than the first episode.[3] Episode 3 was much shorter, lasting about a week, before Episode 4 in 2019–2020 marked a renewed and longer phase lasting about 270 days.[3] During Episode 4, the eruptive style changed from Strombolian activity with lava effusion to lava fountains and then to ash emission probably generated by phreatomagmatic activity, a style not previously observed in Nishinoshima’s documented eruptions.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima’s ecology is shaped by its isolation within the oceanic Ogasawara Islands and by the repeated creation and destruction of habitat on a small volcanic island surface.[4][5] The wider Ogasawara archipelago is noted for island-specific ecosystems, many endemic and rare species, and evolutionary processes such as adaptive radiation and speciation, providing the ecological context for Nishinoshima’s biota.[5] On Nishinoshima, frequent eruptions and marine erosion periodically alter or remove terrestrial habitats, so ecological succession can be repeatedly reset on newly exposed volcanic substrates.[2][5]

The island has been used as a natural site for studying colonization, including research on plant invasion of new volcanic land after eruptions.[3][4] Because new surfaces are initially poor in soil and vegetation, the establishment of plants and animals on Nishinoshima is of interest for understanding early-stage island ecosystem development.[3][4] Common purslane, Portulaca oleracea, was recorded from Nishinoshima, but researchers reported that the island population was eliminated by later eruptive activity after living plants had been collected for laboratory study.[2] Marine ecological development is also monitored around the island, including the establishment of coral reefs and other marine biodiversity around newly formed volcanic terrain.[3]

Nishinoshima is therefore regarded as a disturbance-prone natural laboratory for observing species colonization, ecological succession, and ecosystem resilience in an isolated oceanic-archipelago setting.[3][5] Its conservation value lies in maintaining an undisturbed record of these processes within the Ogasawara Islands, where isolation and varied sources of colonizing biota have produced distinctive island ecosystems.[5]
